utility_details.judge_error: Both wi_skills and wo_skills runs failed to execute any actual GWS tool calls despite the wi_skills agent having access to the persona-sales-ops skill. The verifier confirms that test_gws_call_was_made, test_gws_drive_upload_invoked, and test_gws_sheets_append_invoked all FAILED in both runs. The wi_skills agent read the skill definition (which references gws workflow +meeting-prep, gws drive +upload, etc.) but only simulated these calls in markdown text rather than actually invoking the GWS tools. This is a critical failure of the skill-equipped agent - it labeled sections with workflow names but did not actually execute them as tool calls.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced similar quality outputs overall, with comparable scores (4/7 each). Neither agent executed actual GWS tool calls (Drive upload, Sheets append, Calendar booking), which is the primary differentiator expected between the two runs. The wi_skills agent did read its persona-sales-ops skill definition and structured the output around workflow labels (gws drive +upload, gws sheets +append, gws calendar +create, gws workflow +meeting-prep), but only used them as section headers in the markdown rather than as actual tool invocations. The wo_skills agent produced a slightly more detailed and professional action plan (7663 bytes vs 6658 bytes) with better formatting of the proposal email draft and a more comprehensive risk log. For document quality alone, wo_skills is marginally better. However, the wi_skills output better mimics the expected structure of a skill-driven workflow. In conclusion, neither run is meaningfully more effective than the other since both failed on the core GWS integration requirements, though wi_skills shows better structural alignment with the expected workflow pattern.
